alsoKnownAs: Fundulopanchax sjoestedti (spelling variant — sjoestedti/sjostedti orthographic only; accepted spelling per Catalog of Fishes is sjostedti)

nativeRange.habitat: Temporary swamps, raphia-swamps, and slow-flowing brooks set within swampy coastal rainforest of the Niger Delta and adjacent southwestern Cameroon. The species occupies shallow, heavily vegetated, shaded pools where the water is soft and stained dark by tannins from leaf litter and organic matter.

decorPreferences: dark fine sand or peat-based substrate
dense marginal and floating plants (Java moss, hornwort, Ceratopteris) to diffuse light
driftwood and leaf litter (Indian Almond Leaves) for tannins and cover
peat filtration or blackwater extract to soften and acidify

diet.notes: In the wild the Blue Gularis feeds primarily on invertebrates and small fish, making it a strongly carnivorous species. In the aquarium, live and frozen meaty foods should make up the bulk of the diet: bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, mosquito larvae, and small earthworms are all accepted. Dry or flake foods are generally taken reluctantly and are best treated as an occasional supplement rather than a staple. Any small fish or fry housed in the same tank are at risk of being eaten.

goodWith: robust medium-to-large characins too large to be eaten
peaceful cichlids of similar size (e.g., dwarf cichlids kept in separate territory)
avoidWith: small fish or fry (will be eaten)
other male Blue Gularis (severe conspecific aggression)
guppies, tetras, or any fish small enough to swallow
slow-moving or long-finned species that cannot evade harassment

imageCandidates.notes: Andrewbogott; 3367x1658. Tack-sharp full-color male ('Niger Delta' form), whole animal in frame, showing blue-green iridescent spotting, red-brown bars, orange/yellow fins and elongated caudal/dorsal/anal fin extensions.
Tommy Kronkvist; 3024x2367. Two live males in a planted aquarium (in-situ), sharp and well-lit, showing blue-green flank iridescence, yellow-orange, dark vertical barring and fin extensions.

summary: One of the largest killifish in the hobby at up to 14 cm, the Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjostedti) is a striking non-annual West African species from the Niger Delta. Males carry vivid blue, red, and yellow coloration with elongated fin extensions. Territorial and predatory, it requires careful tank mate selection and a secure, tight-fitting lid.
careNotes:
The Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjostedti) is among the largest killifish kept in the aquarium hobby, with males reaching up to 14 cm and displaying vivid blue-green iridescence on the flanks combined with red-and-yellow patterning and elongated caudal, dorsal, and anal fin extensions. Unlike the better-known annual killifish genera such as Nothobranchius and Austrolebias — whose eggs must be dried out between seasons to hatch — the Blue Gularis is a non-annual species. Its eggs hatch in water without a dry period, and the fish itself is long-lived for a killifish at 3–5 years under good conditions.

The species is native to soft, tannin-stained rainforest swamps, so water parameters should reflect those origins: soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) is strongly advised. Peat filtration or blackwater extract, Indian Almond Leaves, and a dark substrate all help replicate the low-hardness, dimly lit conditions of the Niger Delta habitat. Temperature should be kept at 23–26°C; the cooler end of this range is generally associated with better longevity, and temperatures above 27°C are best avoided.

A minimum tank of 80 L with at least a 90 cm footprint is appropriate for a pair or trio. Dense planting and floating cover serve dual purposes: they diffuse light (the species tends to prefer subdued conditions) and provide refuge for females, which males will pursue persistently. A ratio of 1 male to 2–3 females is strongly advised to spread that pressure. Males are highly territorial toward one another and are generally best not housed together unless the tank is very large with solid visual barriers between territories.

This species is a skilled jumper and will exploit any gap in the aquarium cover. A tight-fitting lid is essential. Because of its predatory nature, tank mates must be large enough that they cannot be swallowed: robust medium-sized characins, Corydoras catfish, and Loricariid plecos are generally suitable companions, while small fish, shrimp, and fry are at serious risk of being eaten.
breedingNotes:
The Blue Gularis is a non-annual, substrate-spawning killifish. A dedicated breeding tank with a peat substrate or spawning mop is recommended, and breeders should be conditioned on live foods such as bloodworms, daphnia, and brine shrimp for 2–4 weeks before pairing. A ratio of 1 male to 2 females is advised to reduce harassment of individual females.

Females deposit eggs in the peat substrate or spawning mop. Individual spawning events produce small numbers, though a productive pair may yield 50–60 eggs per week over a 2–3 week period. Eggs can be incubated two ways: kept in water, they typically hatch in approximately 2–5 weeks; placed semi-dry on damp peat, they require 6 or more weeks before being wetted to prompt hatching. Semi-dry incubation is commonly reported to reduce fungusing, which can be a significant problem — up to 50–75% of eggs may be lost to fungus, and antifungal treatment with methylene blue or acriflavine during incubation is widely advised.

Fry hatch at a relatively large size and grow quickly, accepting micro-worms and baby brine shrimp from the start. Fish do not typically become reliable breeders until close to one year of age, and breeding is generally considered moderately to quite difficult given the egg-handling demands and high fungusing rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Fundulopanchax sjoestedti (Golden Pheasant)
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (12 cm / 4.8 in), temperature (23–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–20°H), minimum tank (90×30×30 cm / ~80 L), native range (Nigeria and Cameroon), habitat (swampy coastal rainforest), diet (small live/frozen foods), temperament (males aggressive toward each other), setup requirements (dark substrate, dense planting, floating plants, tight lid), and taxonomy (formerly Aphyosemion sjoestedti). Spelling variant sjoestedti used in URL/title — same species as sjostedti per Catalog of Fishes.

additional.name: FishBase — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (13 cm SL / 14 cm TL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (5–19 dH), native range (Niger Delta, Nigeria and Cameroon), habitat (temporary swamps, raphia-swamps, slow-flowing brooks in swampy coastal rainforest), taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), diet (trophic level 3.4, invertebrates and small fish), non-migratory freshwater species, IUCN Endangered status.

additional.name: Aquarium Wiki — Fundulopanchax sjostedti
additional.notes: Cross-checked care data. Site was access-restricted during this session; data supplemented from aqua-fish.net and killi.co.uk.

additional.name: British Killifish Association — Fundulopanchax sjoestedti
additional.notes: Specialist killifish source. Confirmed adult male size ~5 in (12.7 cm), temperature preference 70–74°F (21–23°C, noting higher temps shorten lifespan), pH 6.5, hardness 50–80 ppm DH, Niger Delta native range, bottom spawner, trio setup (1M:2F), 50–60 eggs/week over 2–3 weeks, egg incubation 5 weeks in water or 3 months damp-peat, males quarrelsome. Noted that fish don't become reliable breeders until almost one year old.

additional.name: aqua-fish.net — Blue Gularis (Fundulopanchax sjostedti)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 11–13 cm, temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 4–18°N, minimum tank 75 L, lifespan up to 3 years, high-protein live/frozen diet, aggressive and territorial temperament, peat substrate spawning, incubation ~3 weeks at 23–25°C, 1M:2F ratio.

fish.breedingNotes: The Blue Gularis is a non-annual substrate spawner. Spawning is best carried out in a dedicated tank with a peat substrate or spawning mop, with a ratio of one male to two females to reduce pressure on individual females. A productive pair may produce 50–60 eggs per week over a 2–3 week period, though individual batches tend to be small. Eggs can be incubated in two ways: kept in water, they typically hatch in 2–5 weeks; incubated semi-dry on damp peat, they take 6 or more weeks before being wetted to trigger hatching. The semi-dry method is commonly reported to reduce fungusing, which is a significant concern — up to 50–75% of eggs may be lost to fungus, and antifungal treatment with methylene blue or acriflavine during incubation is widely advised. Fish are not typically reliable breeders until close to one year of age. Fry hatch at a relatively large size and grow quickly, accepting micro-worms and baby brine shrimp from the outset.
